messages.content:
Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

Do NOT capture (these become persistent self-imposed constraints that bite you later when the environment changes):
  • Environment-dependent failures: missing binaries, fresh-install errors, post-migration path mismatches, 'command not found', unconfigured credentials, uninstalled packages. The user can fix these — they are not durable rules.
  • Negative claims about tools or features ('browser tools do not work', 'X tool is broken', 'cannot use Y from execute_code'). These harden into refusals the agent cites against itself for months after the actual problem was fixed.
  • Session-specific transient errors that resolved before the conversation ended. If retrying worked, the lesson is the retry pattern, not the original failure.
  • One-off task narratives. A user asking 'summarize today's market' or 'analyze this PR' is not a class of work that warrants a skill.

If a tool failed because of setup state, capture the FIX (install command, config step, env var to set) under an existing setup or troubleshooting skill — never 'this tool does not work' as a standalone constraint.

Covers layout patterns, theme system, visual asset inventory/cleanup, image gap analysis, auth cookie debugging, deployment follow-through, and post-deployment client readiness review." version: 1.6.0 tags: [nextjs, ecommerce, admin, audit, tailwind, layout, sidebar, supabase, auth, cloudflare, org-restructuring, package-extraction] --- # Ecommerce Codebase Audit Inspect and improve a Next.js e-commerce admin panel. Focus on layout consistency, theme application, page structure, image assets, and auth flow. ## Audit Checklist ### Layout Consistency - [ ] AdminShell lives in `app/admin/layout.tsx` (centralized layout), NOT in individual pages - [ ] Run `grep -rn "AdminShell" app/admin/ --include="page.tsx"` — should return zero results - [ ] `AdminShell` includes the sidebar navigation (responsive: hidden on mobile, fixed on desktop) - [ ] Sidebar uses the same links and active-state highlighting across all pages - [ ] All pages render inside `` via the root layout, not per-page wrappers ### Theme System The admin theme uses emerald/zinc on a dark background (#0a0a0b / #0f0f10): ```tsx // Tailwind classes to look for bg-[#0f0f10] // sidebar + card backgrounds bg-zinc-800/60 // borders text-emerald-400 // active text bg-emerald-500/10 // active background text-zinc-400 // inactive text hover:bg-zinc-800/40 // hover state ``` - [ ] All pages use the same color tokens (not hardcoded different colors) - [ ] Active page shows `bg-emerald-500/10 text-emerald-400` with indicator dot - [ ] Inactive pages use `text-zinc-400 hover:text-zinc-200 hover:bg-zinc-800/40` - [ ] All interactive elements have `transition-all` for smooth hover states - [ ] Borders use `zinc-800/60` or `zinc-700/60` — never crude `gray-200` or default tailwind borders ### Page Structure Each admin page follows this pattern. IMPORTANT: AdminShell is NOT imported by individual pages — it is applied by app/admin/layout.tsx: ```tsx 'use client' import { useAdminAuth } from "@/components/admin/admin-layout" import { useState, useEffect } from "react" export default function PageName() { const { authed } = useAdminAuth() if (!authed) return null return (
{/* content — no AdminShell wrapper needed */}
) } ``` - [ ] Pages use `{ authed } = useAdminAuth()` for auth guard - [ ] Pages do NOT import AdminShell (layout handles it) - [ ] Pages use `fetch("/api/admin/*")` for data (not direct Supabase client) - [ ] Data fetched via `/api/admin/` routes that use service_role key server-side ### Admin UI Design Standards (El Viajero) When building or redesigning admin data table pages, follow these standards from the El Viajero productos page redesign: #### Table Column Widths Use fixed Tailwind width classes on `` elements so the table fills properly without huddling: | Column Type | Width | Notes | |------------|-------|-------| | Image/thumbnail | `w-12` | Small square, package icon placeholder when missing | | Name | fill | Remaining space, with brand as sub-label | | Price | `w-28` | Font-semibold, optionally show price_before as strikethrough | | Stock | `w-16` | Colored dot indicator (emerald >10, amber >0, red =0) | | Category | `w-28` | Pill badge style | | Actions | `w-40` | Right-aligned icon buttons | #### Action Buttons (NEVER text links) Replace text links like "Editar | Duplicar | Eliminar" with lucide icon buttons: | Action | Icon | Hover Color | Hover BG | |--------|------|-------------|----------| | Edit | `Pencil` | `text-blue-400` | `bg-blue-500/10` | | Duplicate | `Copy` | `text-amber-400` | `bg-amber-500/10` | | Delete | `Trash2` | `text-red-400` | `bg-red-500/10` | Each icon button: `rounded-lg p-1.5` with `opacity-70 group-hover:opacity-100` on the container for subtle reveal. Add `title` attribute for tooltip context. #### Search Bar - Place below the header row, prominent, with a `Search` lucide icon on the left - Max-width `max-w-md` so it doesn't over-extend - Include a clear button (`X` icon) when text is present - Use `rounded-xl` and `bg-zinc-900/50` with `border-zinc-800` - Focus ring: `focus:border-emerald-500/50 focus:ring-1 focus:ring-emerald-500/20` - Filter results locally by name, category, and brand #### Stock Indicator Show stock count with colored dot: ```tsx 10 ? "text-emerald-400" : stock > 0 ? "text-amber-400" : "text-red-400" }`}> 10 ? "bg-emerald-400" : stock > 0 ? "bg-amber-400" : "bg-red-400" }`} /> {stock} ``` #### Pagination Full pagination bar with: - Page range display: `"1-20 de 100 productos"` - Prev/Next buttons with `ChevronLeft`/`ChevronRight` icons, disabled state - Page number buttons with active state (`emerald` bg highlight) - Ellipsis (`...`) for gaps - Each page button: `w-7 h-7 rounded-lg text-xs font-medium` #### Empty State Use `Package` icon (or page-appropriate lucide icon) centered: ```tsx

{search ? "No results" : "No items yet"}

{!search && }
``` #### Toast Notifications Fixed top-right toast for success/error feedback: ```tsx
{type === "success" ? : } {text}
``` #### Image Thumbnails - Fixed size `h-9 w-9 rounded-lg border` - Clickable to open full-image modal - Fallback: `Package` lucide icon on `bg-zinc-800/50` when `image_url` is missing or is `.svg` - Active ring: `hover:ring-2 hover:ring-emerald-500/40` #### Inline Edit Mode When a row enters edit mode: - Replace display elements with `input` elements using `rounded-lg bg-zinc-800` - Use `focus:outline-none focus:border-emerald-500/50` on all inputs - Show Save/Cancel buttons replacing the action icons - Save button: `bg-emerald-600` (green); Cancel: `border-zinc-700/60` (outlined) ### AdminShell Component Located in `@/components/admin/admin-layout` (or wherever it is imported from). The AdminShell provides: - Sidebar navigation with 4 groups: Operaciones, Catálogo, Marketing, Configuración - Mobile responsiveness (hidden sidebar on mobile, toggle with hamburger) - Auth guard via `useAdminAuth` - Consistent background and layout padding The AdminShell is imported by `app/admin/layout.tsx` (the root admin layout) — NOT by individual pages. ### Data Table Loading States Always implement ALL three states: | State | Visual | Implementation | |-------|--------|---------------| | Loading | Skeleton rows with pulse animation | `animate-pulse` on placeholder divs matching cell shape | | Empty | Icon + text + optional CTA | Lucide icon + message + Create button | | Data | Full table with hover states | `group hover:bg-zinc-800/30` on each `` | For the loading skeleton, match the eventual cell content's shape and count: ```tsx {loading && Array.from({ length: 6 }).map((_, i) => (
))} ``` ### Search Bar Design (Detail) ```tsx
{ setSearch(e.target.value); setPage(1) }} placeholder="Search placeholder..." className="w-full rounded-xl border border-zinc-800 bg-zinc-900/50 pl-9 pr-9 py-2.5 text-sm text-white placeholder-zinc-500 focus:outline-none focus:border-emerald-500/50 focus:ring-1 focus:ring-emerald-500/20 transition-all" /> {search && ( )}
``` ### Bulk Operations Panel When a page has bulk operations (e.g. price update), use a collapsible panel: ```tsx {showBulk && (

Title

)} ``` ### Auth Pattern ```tsx 'use client' import { useAdminAuth } from "@/components/admin/admin-layout" const { authed } = useAdminAuth() if (!authed) return null ``` Note: `AdminShell` is NOT imported here — it comes from `app/admin/layout.tsx`. Login stores session in localStorage + elviajero_admin_token cookie. useAdminAuth tries localStorage → Supabase SSR cookie → custom cookie, all via /api/auth with Authorization: Bearer header. ## Product SEO: generateMetadata Pattern Every e-commerce product page needs dynamic `generateMetadata` for search engines and social sharing. The pattern is non-obvious when products are fetched client-side (useEffect from Supabase) rather than server-side. ### The Problem In most Next.js e-commerce apps, product pages are client components that fetch data via `useEffect` from Supabase. This means: - No server-side `` or `<meta>` tags — pages get the default layout metadata - Social shares (WhatsApp, Facebook, Twitter) show generic OG tags, not the product info - Google sees no product-specific title/description ### The Pattern Create a server-side `export async function generateMetadata` in `app/producto/[slug]/page.tsx` that fetches product data independently of the client component: ```tsx import { createClient } from "@ai-whisperers/auth/supabase/server" import type { Metadata } from "next" const staticProducts = c.home?.productCatalog?.products || [] function slugify(s: string) { return s.toLowerCase().replace(/[^a-z0-9áéíóúñü]+/g, "-").replace(/-+$/, "") } export async function generateMetadata({ params }: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }): Promise<Metadata> { const { slug } = await params let name = "", description = "", imageUrl = "", price = "" // Try DB first — fetch all and match by slugified name try { const supabase = await createClient() const { data: allData } = await supabase.from("ej_products").select("name, description, image_url, price") if (allData) { const match = allData.find((p: any) => slugify(p.name) === slug) if (match) { name = match.name description = match.description?.substring(0, 160) || match.name imageUrl = match.image_url price = match.price } } } catch {} // Fallback to static content JSON if (!name) { const staticProd = staticProducts.find((p: any) => slugify(p.name) === slug) if (staticProd) { name = staticProd.name description = (staticProd.description || staticProd.name).substring(0, 160) imageUrl = staticProd.imageUrl || "" price = staticProd.price } } if (!name) return {} return { title: `${name} | Store Name — Tagline`, description, openGraph: { title, description, url: `https://store.com/producto/${slug}`, type: "product", images: [{ url: imageUrl || "/images/og-default.webp", width: 1200, height: 630, alt: name }], }, twitter: { card: "summary_large_image", title, description, images: [...] }, other: { "product:price:amount": price, "product:price:currency": "PYG" }, } } ``` ### Key Techniques 1. **slugify() matching** — Most small e-commerce DBs don't have a `slug` column. Products are matched by slugifying the `name` field client-side. The server-side metadata function must use the same algorithm. 2. **DB → Static fallback** — Try Supabase first (has the most current data), fall back to static content JSON. Never show a product page without metadata. 3. **Empty return** — If no product found for the slug (e.g., invalid URL), return `{}` — Next.js falls back to root layout metadata cleanly. 4. **`params` is a Promise** — Next.js 15+ requires `await params`. Important for both `generateMetadata` and the default page export. 5. **Price as meta tag** — Add `other: { "product:price:amount", "product:price:currency" }` so social platforms and price comparison tools can extract it. 6. **OG image fallback** — Use `.webp` format in the fallback image path (not `.png`) to save bandwidth. ### Verification ```bash # Check metadata is live curl -s https://store.com/producto/some-product | grep -oP '<title>[^<]+' curl -s https://store.com/producto/some-product | grep -oP ' { const b = new Set() products.forEach((p: any) => { if (p.brand) b.add(p.brand) }) return Array.from(b).sort() }, [products]) // Filter logic if (brandFilter) { result = result.filter((p) => p.brand === brandFilter) } // Dependency array // Add `brandFilter` to useMemo dependency array // UI — insert between Category and Sort dropdowns {brands.length > 0 && (
)} ``` ### Key Points - Brands are extracted dynamically from product data — no hardcoded brand list - Brands dropdown is conditionally rendered: only show when there are brands to filter - The grid layout may need to expand from 5 to 6 columns to fit the brand dropdown - Brands are sorted A-Z for consistent UX ## Auth Flow & Cookie Debugging ### The Core Trap: Two Auth Paths Crossing In Next.js + Supabase e-commerce projects, there are usually TWO auth paths that can conflict: **Path A — Consumer auth (useAuth):** - Login page calls `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()` directly from the browser client - Supabase's `@supabase/ssr` browser client sets the session cookie automatically - `/mi-cuenta` reads session via `supabase.auth.getSession()` — works because cookie exists **Path B — Admin auth (useAdminAuth / /api/auth):** - Login page calls `/api/auth` with `action: "login"` - API route creates an SSR client with `createServerClient()`, but often has `setAll() {}` empty - **The session cookie never gets set on the response** — the API returns tokens, but no cookie - Admin layout falls back to localStorage or custom cookie **Common failure modes:** 1. **Login works (gets tokens) but /mi-cuenta shows unauthenticated** — consumer login page fetches `/api/auth` instead of calling Supabase directly. Fix: use `useAuth().login()` (Supabase browser client) which sets the correct session cookie. 2. **Login works but /admin redirects back to /login** — admin layout's `useAdminAuth()` calls `/api/auth?action=me` with a token from localStorage, but the API route's token verification path fails. Fix: ensure the API route can verify tokens against Supabase REST API (not just SSR cookies). ### Fix Checklist - [ ] Consumer login uses Supabase browser client directly (`supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()`), NOT a custom `/api/auth` endpoint - [ ] After successful login, store session tokens in `localStorage` for admin layout fallback: `localStorage.setItem("elviajero_admin_session", JSON.stringify({ access_token, refresh_token }))` - [ ] Admin layout checks localStorage first, then Supabase SSR cookie, then custom cookie — triple fallback - [ ] API route `action: "me"` verifies tokens via `fetch(${supabaseUrl}/auth/v1/user)` not `supabase.auth.getUser()` - [ ] Service-role client for profile queries uses `createClient(url, serviceRoleKey)` — passing service key as second arg, not in headers - [ ] Role check queries DB profiles table (not JWT claim) — `profiles.role` not `user.role` ### Debugging Auth Issues ```bash # Check if API route is reachable curl -sX POST https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/api/auth \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"action":"login","email":"[REDACTED_EMAIL]","password":"test"}' | head -c 200 # Check if session cookie is set curl -sv https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/mi-cuenta 2>&1 | grep -i 'set-cookie' # Check server logs docker service logs elviajero-comercio_web --tail 30 --no-task-ids ``` ## Visual Asset Workflow ### Full Asset Overhaul Pattern When doing a complete visual assets redesign (inventory, generation, deployment): #### Phase 1: Inventory ```bash # List all files on disk find public/images -type f | sort # List all code references grep -rohP '"/images/[^"'"'"']+' --include='*.{tsx,ts,json,css}' | sed 's/^"//' | sort -u # Cross-reference: 3 outcomes per image # 1. Referenced + on disk = OK # 2. Referenced + missing = FIX (add file) # 3. On disk + no reference = ORPHAN (delete) # Size audit — small = programmatic (low quality) find public/images -name "*.png" -size -50k ``` #### Phase 2: Categorization | Type | Size threshold | Keep as | Upgrade? | |------|---------------|---------|----------| | SVGs | varies | Vector | Only if they're emoji text | | Real photos | >50KB | PNG | No | | Programmatic renders | <50KB | PNG | Yes — regenerate with richer Pillow composites | | Icons | 4-8KB | PNG | Intentionally small — minimalist silhouettes | | Hero backgrounds | <20KB | PNG | Yes — add atmosphere, silhouettes, gradients | #### Phase 3: Generation (no AI API available) Use Pillow for progressive enhancement rounds: **Round 1 — Placeholders:** ```python # Sky gradient + bokeh circles + category silhouettes # Write to a generate script, deploy, iterate ``` **Round 2 — Richer details:** - Tree silhouettes, stars, glowing tent, moon - River reflections, fishing rod, sun at horizon - Mountain trails, hiker with backpack - Headlight beams, road lines, car approaching - Dust clouds, motorcycle, fence - Fields, barn, sun, clouds **Round 3 — AI (if REPLICATE_API_TOKEN or equivalent available):** ```python if call_replicate(prompt, w, h, path): print("AI generated") else: render_fallback(path) # keep best Pillow version ``` **Round 4 — Self-critique:** After deploying, review each asset and write a brutal critique doc. Then regenerate the worst offenders in the next round. #### Phase 4: Deployment Pitfalls **Cloudflare cache (IMPORTANT):** - CF sets `cache-control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` — 1 YEAR cache - No CF API key? Only option is **rename the file** (logo.png → logo-v2.png) - Then update ALL code references to point to new filename - Verify with curl, not browser: `curl -sk URL/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B"` - Then tell the user to hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) **Docker layer cache:** - If files exist locally but return 404 on deploy, the Docker COPY was cached - Fix: `docker build --no-cache` or touch a file in the COPY layer **Verify the right file is served:** ```bash # Check HTML references curl -sk https://example.com/ | grep -oP '(logo|favicon)-v2' # Check file directly curl -sk https://example.com/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B" ``` ### Key Project-Specific Patterns (El Viajero) - Product images: `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/products/*.png` (34 products) - Category backgrounds (dynamic SVG): `/images/categories/${slug}.svg` — slug values in `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` - Category heroes: heroImage prop → `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` → `/images/marketing/hero-*.png` - Hero carousel: content-driven array in `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/hero-carousel-*.png` - Testimonials: `"image"` field in `content/es.json` → `/images/testimonials/avatar-*.png` - Logo: `header.tsx` → `/images/logo.png` (or `logo-v2.png` after cache-bust) - Favicon: `layout.tsx` → `/images/favicon.png` (or `favicon-v2.png`) - OG image: `layout.tsx` → `/images/og-viajero.svg` - PWA icons: `manifest.json` → `/images/icon-192.png`, `/images/icon-512.png` ### Generation Recipes **Category icons (128x128):** Create a Python script with `ImageDraw` — tent, fish, umbrella, backpack, car, motorcycle, barn silhouettes on green circle background. These are intentionally small (4-8KB). **Hero banners (1920x600):** Create scene composites: 1. Sky gradient (top to bottom, warm/cool depending on scene) 2. Ground layer (dark rectangle) 3. Scene elements (tree silhouettes, stars, moon, tent, river, fishing rod, mountains, road, headlights, barn, fence, sun) 4. Bokeh overlay (random circles with low alpha) 5. Gaussian blur (radius 0.3-0.5) for polish **Testimonial avatars (200x200):** If no AI available, create colored circles with basic face features (eyes, smile, hair shape, shirt). Tell the client these are placeholders for real customer photos. **PWA icons:** Render from the logo at 192x192 and 512x512 with anti-aliasing. ## Multi-Locale Content Updates (PAB Sites) When updating content across all locales (en/es/nl/de) in a PAB (Paragu-AI Builder) project: ### Safe approach vs. pitfalls 1. **Python JSON manipulation is safer than `patch` tool for large content changes.** The `patch` tool works well for small string replacements, but when replacing JSON blocks spanning 50+ lines, it can match the wrong text, leave orphaned content, and produce valid-but-wrong JSON. This happened when replacing the `processPage` section — patch matched a subset, leaving trailing orphan that was syntactically valid but structurally wrong. 2. **When `patch` goes wrong: stop, don't stack.** If the first patch produces unexpected results (orphan content, duplicated sections, lost keys), do NOT apply more patches on top. Switch immediately to Python `json.load()/json.dump()` — read the broken file, fix the data structure in memory, and write it back clean. Stacking patches compounds the mistakes and makes the file increasingly fragile to further edits. The `processPage` fix required three patches to clean up what one wrong match did. 3. **`patch` can match content from a previously-applied patch** — If a file was already modified, the old_string might not match what's actually in the file. Always read the file fresh before patching, and verify with a direct key inspection after (`python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('f')); print(d.get('targetKey',{}).keys())"`). 4. **Shell variable expansion fails in `execute_code`'s multi-line Python strings** — `for f in $FILES; do python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('$f'))"; done` breaks because `$f` isn't expanded inside the Python string. Use explicit file paths, a temp script, or Python's own `glob` module instead. 5. **Always validate JSON structure after content changes, not just syntax.** A file can be valid JSON but structurally wrong (missing keys, wrong step counts, orphan content). After validation: ```bash python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('file'))" # syntax check ``` Also verify structural integrity: ```python d["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] # should have 5 steps d["home"]["services"]["groups"] # should have 3 groups d["aboutPage"]["story"]["paragraphs"] # should have 3 paragraphs ``` 6. **When deleting orphaned content from a botched patch, read the file with JSON parser first**, then find the structural key that contains the orphan data. Delete it from the Python dict and re-write. Don't try to surgically remove lines with patch. 7. **Process step consistency rule:** The same process narrative lives in 6 places across the site. Update ALL of these together in the same script block — missing any one creates inconsistency: - `home.process.steps` (homepage) - `processPage.process.steps` (process page) - `landingInversor.process.steps`, `landingTrust.process.steps`, `landingLifestyle.process.steps`, `landingEmpresa.process.steps` (LinkedIn ad landing pages) 8. **Process for adding new content sections across locales:** - Step 1: Define the content data structure in a Python dict with all localizations - Step 2: Load each locale file, apply transformations, write back - Step 3: Validate JSON syntax for all files - Step 4: Verify structural keys match expectations (count groups, steps, paragraphs) ```python for lang in ["en", "es", "nl", "de"]: with open(f"content/{lang}.json") as f: d = json.load(f) d["parentSection"]["newKey"] = translations[lang] with open(f"content/{lang}.json", "w") as f: json.dump(d, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) ``` 9. **Section structure for the 'Our Services' pattern:** ```python data["home"]["services"] = { "eyebrow": "Our Services", "title": "Section title", "groups": [ {"id": "group-id", "title": "Group Name", "subtitle": "Group subtitle.", "items": [{"title": "Service", "description": "Desc.", "icon": "IconName"}]} ] } ``` 10. **Section structure for the 'Story' pattern:** ```python data["aboutPage"]["story"] = { "title": "Our Story", "eyebrow": "Tagline", "paragraphs": ["Para 1.", "Para 2.", "Para 3."] } ``` 11. **Section structure for the 'Process steps' pattern:** ```python data["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] = [ {"number": 1, "title": "Step Name", "description": "Long description.", "icon": "IconName", "image": {"$img": "process.key"}, "duration": "Week 1"} ] ``` 12. **Landing page Calendly URL pattern:** ```python base = "https://calendly.com/nexaparaguay/consulta" utm = f"utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign={campaign}" url = f"{base}?{utm}&utm_content=cta-{placement}-{lang}" ``` ## Deployment ### Docker Deployment After modifying content assets (images, content JSON, or static files): 1. **Rebuild** — `npm run build` catches missing images and type errors 2. **Rebuild Docker image** — `docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .` 3. **Deploy** — `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio --detach=false` 4. **Verify** — `curl -sI https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/ | head -5` 5. **Force rolling update** — if same image tag, `docker service update --force elviajero-comercio_web` Never assume changes to static content or images are live — Docker rebuild and stack deploy are required. Browser cache is NOT the server — always curl first. ### Production Monitoring After every deploy, add: - Healthcheck endpoint at `/api/health` returning `{status, timestamp, uptime}` - Docker HEALTHCHECK in Dockerfile (every 30s, 40s start grace, 3 retries) - Cron healthcheck via Hermes: every 15m, auto-redeploy on failure - Shell check script: `/root/scripts/check-{project}.sh` ### Docker Build Timeout `docker build` with npm install can take >120s on cold cache. Set terminal timeout to 300s for builds. If it times out, retry — the npm cache is warm on the second run and it will complete faster. #### Next.js `pages/` Folder Conflict (v16+) Next.js 16+ errors with "`pages` and `app` directories should be under the same folder" when a `pages/` data directory exists at project root alongside `src/app/`. Fix: 1. Rename `pages/` to `nexa-pages/` 2. Update all `page.tsx` references from `'pages'` to `'nexa-pages'` 3. Update Dockerfile to copy `nexa-pages/` into the standalone output 4. Run `docker build --no-cache` after rename (Docker layer cache may use old path) #### Docker Standalone + Data Files Next.js `output: "standalone"` produces a `server.js` that does `process.chdir(__dirname)` — so `process.cwd()` points to `.next/standalone/`, not the app root. Data files read at runtime via `readFileSync(path.join(process.cwd(), ...))` must be copied into `.next/standalone/`: ```dockerfile COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./ COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./.next/standalone/nexa-pages COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./.next/standalone/content ``` Without this, the container runs fine but returns static HTML with fallback data because `readFileSync` silently fails and the catch block returns defaults. #### The "Welcome" Bug (Static SSG Fallback) When a PAB page always renders "Welcome" regardless of content changes, the root cause is in the page component's fallback chain: ```tsx // BROKEN: home.json has 'titleKey' not 'title' — always falls through {homePage.title || content?.title || 'Welcome'} // FIXED: use siteName from the locale JSON {content?.siteName || 'Nexa Paraguay'} ``` Check that `getPages()` and `getContent()` actually read files via `readFileSync`/`readdirSync` at build time. The `catch` block silently swallowing the error is the symptom — verify the files are accessible during the Docker build stage. #### `window.gtag` Type Conflicts When a project uses `declare global { interface Window { gtag?: any } }` and imports from `@ai-whisperers/seo/analytics` (which also declares it), you get: ``` Type error: All declarations of 'gtag' must have identical modifiers. ``` **Fix:** Use `(window as any).gtag` everywhere instead of declaring `interface Window`: ```typescript const w = window as any w.gtag("event", "page_view", data) ``` Apply to all GA4 tracking files: `components/analytics.tsx`, `lib/analytics.ts`. ## Traefik + Docker Swarm Routing Setup When deploying a new Next.js client site to an existing Docker Swarm + Traefik infrastructure (paragu-ai.com VPS at 72.61.44.159): ### 1. DNS Record (Cloudflare) Add an A record in the Cloudflare dashboard for the paragu-ai.com zone: ``` Type: A Name: {site-name} Content: 72.61.44.159 Proxy status: Proxied (orange cloud) ``` ### 2. docker-compose.yml (Traefik Labels) The template's `docker-compose.yml` has superspuma's labels. Update them: ```yaml services: web: build: . image: {site-name}:prod ports: ["3000"] networks: [agent-net] deploy: mode: replicated replicas: 2 resources: limits: { memory: 512M, cpus: '0.5' } reservations: { memory: 128M, cpus: '0.1' } labels: - "traefik.enable=true" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.entrypoints=websecure" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.rule=Host(`{site-name}.paragu-ai.com`)" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.tls=true" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver" - "traefik.http.services.{site-name}.loadbalancer.server.port=3000" - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net" networks: agent-net: external: true ``` Pitfall: the `image` field in docker-compose.yml must match the `docker build -t` tag. ### 3. Deploy (Manual on VPS) ```bash ssh root@72.61.44.159 cd /root/{repo-name} git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) # for @ai-whisperers npm packages npm ci --legacy-peer-deps npm run build docker build -t {site-name}:prod . docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml {site-name} ``` After deploy, verify: ```bash curl -sI https://{site-name}.paragu-ai.com/ | grep "HTTP/" ``` ### 4. Docker Build GITHUB_TOKEN Issue The standard Docker multi-stage build (template's Dockerfile) runs `npm install` inside the build container but doesn't have access to the GITHUB_TOKEN for @ai-whisperers GitHub Packages registry. **Two solutions:** **Option A (simple):** Build on the host, then use a production-only Dockerfile that copies the already-built output: ```dockerfile # Dockerfile.prod — used after `npm run build` on host FROM node:20-alpine AS runner WORKDIR /app ENV NODE_ENV=production NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 PORT=3000 HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0" RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs . . USER nextjs EXPOSE 3000 CMD ["node_modules/.bin/next", "start"] ``` ```bash # On VPS: npm run build # host build has GITHUB_TOKEN docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t site:prod . # just ships node_modules + .next docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml site ``` **Option B (Docker build-arg):** Pass the token as a build arg and set it in the .npmrc at build time: ```dockerfile FROM node:20-alpine AS deps WORKDIR /app COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./ ARG NODE_AUTH_TOKEN RUN echo "//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=${NODE_AUTH_TOKEN}" >> .npmrc && \ npm install --legacy-peer-deps ``` ```bash docker build --build-arg NODE_AUTH_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) -t site:prod . ``` **Pitfall:** Docker BuildKit blocks ARG values from ENV for security ("Do not use ARG or ENV instructions for sensitive data"). Option A is more reliable. ### 5. CI/CD Workflow The reusable ci-cd workflows (Ai-Whisperers/ci-cd) may not be accessible from new repos due to permissions. Example standalone deploy.yml as fallback: ```yaml name: Deploy on: push: branches: [main] jobs: deploy: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: node-version: '20' cache: 'npm' - run: npm ci --legacy-peer-deps - run: npm run build - name: Setup SSH run: | mkdir -p ~/.ssh echo "${{ secrets.VPS_KEY }}" > ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 ssh-keyscan -H ${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts 2>/dev/null || true - name: Deploy via SSH run: | ssh ${{ secrets.VPS_USER }}@${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} \ "cd /root/{repo-name} && git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main && \ export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) && npm ci --legacy-peer-deps && npm run build && \ docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t {site-name}:prod . && \ docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml {site-name}" ``` ## Google Maps Browser Navigation for Search-Engine-Blocked Research When search engines (Google, DuckDuckGo, Bing, Brave) block automated queries with CAPTCHAs/IP blocks, use browser_navigate to Google Maps as a workaround: ### Pattern 1. Navigate to Google Maps search URL directly: ```bash https://www.google.com/maps/search/{query}+{location} ``` 2. The browser snapshot shows results as readable `article` elements with: - Company name (clickable) - Rating (stars) - Address - Phone number - Hours - Website link (when available) 3. Click a result to view more details in the side panel. 4. To refine, clear the combobox and type a new query, then press Enter. ### Queries That Work Spanish-language queries work better for Paraguayan businesses: ``` "constructora civil Asuncion Paraguay" "vidrio templado laminado fachada Asuncion" "fachada vidrio aluminio Asuncion Paraguay" "desarrolladora inmobiliaria Asuncion Paraguay" "arquitecto Asuncion Paraguay fachada" ``` ### Limitations - Only shows ~7 results per view (Google Maps limited view without login) - No API access to extract full data programmatically - Phone numbers and emails must be copied manually from snapshots - Results are biased toward Google's ranking, not comprehensive - Works for Paraguay because Google Maps has good coverage there ## B2B Lead-Gen Conversion (e-commerce template → B2B site) When converting the e-commerce template (template-nextjs-client) into a B2B lead-generation site (construction materials, industrial supply, wholesale), follow this pattern: ### What to REMOVE from the template ``` B2C pages to delete: app/tienda/ → product store with cart/checkout app/envio/ → shipping info app/promociones/ → promotions app/producto/[slug]/ → individual product pages (replace with /productos/ catalog) B2C components to delete: search-autocomplete.tsx → no product search for B2B dark-mode-toggle.tsx → B2B stays light theme payment-methods.tsx → no payments on lead-gen site quick-order.tsx → no quick ordering product-card.tsx → replace with spec-card pattern hero-carousel.tsx → use static hero for B2B promo-carousel.tsx → no promos bottom-nav.tsx → not needed for B2B mobile empty-state.tsx → no shopping experience recently-viewed.tsx → no browsing history wishlist.ts / lib/ → no wishlists ``` ### Post-Deployment Content Audit / Roast Pattern After deploying a converted B2B site (or any new client site from template), run a systematic content audit by browsing every page live and checking for: 1. **Template remnants**: Any text/images from the original template site that leaked through (Fun4Me brand, superspuma references, wrong Instagram handles). Check /contacto, /nosotros, /faq, footer especially — these are the most commonly overlooked. 2. **Content that doesn't match the business type**: - B2B construction site still showing e-commerce order tracking - Professional services site saying "bienestar íntimo" (sex shop) - Law firm showing shipping/returns information 3. **Placeholder phone numbers**: Any hardcoded `WA_PHONE = "[REDACTED_PHONE]"` (Fun4Me's number) that wasn't replaced. 4. **Broken links**: Links pointing to removed pages (/tienda, /envio, /producto/[slug]). 5. **Empty portfolio/projects**: "Coming soon" or "próximamente" on a B2B site that needs trust signals. Replace with a CTA-driven alternative. ### Content Audit / Roast Methodology Browser every page on the live site using browser_navigate and check: | Page | Common Issues | |------|---------------| | /contacto | Fun4Me Instagram, returns/orders/shipping links, wrong phone | | /nosotros | "¿Por qué Fun4Me?" section, sex shop story, purple heart, /tienda links | | /faq | Generic questions instead of industry-specific technical answers | | /proyectos | "Coming soon" instead of real portfolio | | /productos | Emoji icons instead of real product images | | /blog | No images, missing headers, broken formatting | After audit, fix the 3 worst pages (usually contacto, nosotros, faq) in one commit, then re-deploy and verify. ### Post-Deployment Content Audit / Roast Pattern After deploying a converted B2B site (or any new client site from template), run a systematic content audit by browsing every page live and checking for: 1. **Template remnants**: Any text/images from the original template site that leaked through (Fun4Me brand, superspuma references, wrong Instagram handles). function.arguments: {"action":"patch","name":"ecommerce-codebase-audit","old_string":"### Fix Checklist - [ ] Consumer login uses Supabase browser client directly (`supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()`), NOT a custom `/api/auth` endpoint - [ ] After successful login, store session tokens in `localStorage` for admin layout fallback: `localStorage.setItem("elviajero_admin_session", JSON.stringify({ access_token, refresh_token }))` - [ ] Admin layout checks localStorage first, then Supabase SSR cookie, then custom cookie — triple fallback - [ ] API route `action: "me"` verifies tokens via `fetch(${supabaseUrl}/auth/v1/user)` not `supabase.auth.getUser()` - [ ] Service-role client for profile queries uses `createClient(url, serviceRoleKey)` — passing service key as second arg, not in headers - [ ] Role check queries DB profiles table (not JWT claim) — `profiles.role` not `user.role`","new_string":"### Fix Checklist - [ ] Consumer login uses Supabase browser client directly (`supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()`), NOT a custom `/api/auth` endpoint - [ ] After successful login, store session tokens in `localStorage` for admin layout fallback: `localStorage.setItem("elviajero_admin_session", JSON.stringify({ access_token, refresh_token }))` - [ ] Admin layout checks localStorage first, then Supabase SSR cookie, then custom cookie — triple fallback - [ ] API route `action: "me"` verifies tokens via `fetch(${supabaseUrl}/auth/v1/user)` not `supabase.auth.getUser()` - [ ] Service-role client for profile queries uses `createClient(url, serviceRoleKey)` — passing service key as second arg, not in headers - [ ] Role check queries DB profiles table (not JWT claim) — `profiles.role` not `user.role` ## Supabase Auth Error Translation (LATAM Sites) Supabase returns auth errors in English ("Invalid login credentials", "Email not confirmed", "User already registered"). For Paraguayan/LATAM clients, these must be translated to Spanish in the frontend before display. ### The Pattern In the auth context (e.g. `packages/auth/auth-context.tsx` or `lib/auth.ts`), intercept the error from `signInWithPassword` and map it: ```tsx const errorMap: Record = { "Invalid login credentials": "Credenciales incorrectas", "Email not confirmed": "Email no confirmado. Revisá tu bandeja de entrada.", "User already registered": "Ya existe una cuenta con este email.", "Password should be at least 6 characters": "La contraseña debe tener al menos 6 caracteres.", } try { const { data, error } = await supabase.auth.signInWithPassword({ email, password }) if (error) throw new Error(errorMap[error.message] || error.message) // ... success } catch (err: any) { setError(err.message) // Spanish message } ``` ### Key Points - Map the **exact** English string Supabase returns — check `error.message` in a test first - Keep a fallback to the raw English message for unmapped errors (don't swallow them) - This pattern applies to `signInWithPassword`, `signUp`, `resetPasswordForEmail`, and `updateUser` - Do NOT try to configure Supabase locale — the GoTrue server doesn't respect i18n params; translation must happen client-side ## Product Catalog Migration: Static JSON → Supabase DB When an e-commerce site starts with products hardcoded in `content/es.json` but needs to scale to real client data, migrate the catalog to Supabase and make the frontend read from DB at runtime. ### Migration Steps 1. **Audit existing data** - Check if products already exist in Supabase: `select count(*) from ej_products` - If they do, decide: merge or truncate + re-seed 2. **Normalize categories** Client-provided categories often have inconsistencies. Normalize BEFORE seeding: | Raw Client Input | Normalized Category | |------------------|---------------------| | Pezca / pesca / fishing | Pesca | | electronica / Electronica | Electrónica | | accesorios pers. / Accesorios Personales | Accesorios Personales | | Equipo p/ Auto / accesorios moto | Accesorios para Vehículos | | Camping / campamento | Camping | ```typescript function normalizeCategory(raw: string): string { const map: Record = { "pezca": "Pesca", "pesca": "Pesca", "fishing": "Pesca", "electronica": "Electrónica", "Electronica": "Electrónica", "accesorios pers.": "Accesorios Personales", "equipo p/ auto": "Accesorios para Vehículos", "accesorios moto": "Accesorios para Vehículos", "camping": "Camping", "campamento": "Camping", } return map[raw.trim().toLowerCase()] || raw.trim() } ``` 3. **Truncate and seed** ```bash # Truncate existing products (cascades to order_items if FK exists) psql "$SUPABASE_DB_URL" -c "TRUNCATE TABLE ej_products RESTART IDENTITY CASCADE;" ``` Then run a seed script that inserts the normalized products. See `references/supabase-product-seeding-pattern.md` for a full seed script template. 4. **Update frontend to read from DB** - In the page component (e.g. `app/tienda/page.tsx`), fetch products server-side or client-side from Supabase - Mark the page as dynamic: `export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"` - The `tienda-content.tsx` component should accept `dbProducts` and merge with any static fallback (static fallback should be empty after migration) 5. **Update content/es.json** - Empty the `home.productCatalog.products` array: `"products": []` - Update `home.productCatalog.categories` to match the normalized category list - This prevents the static JSON from shadowing the DB data 6. **Verify the mapping** - Ensure every product from DB has an `id` field mapped correctly for cart operations: ```tsx const products = dbData.map((p: any) => ({ id: p.id, // CRITICAL: cart needs this name: p.name, price: p.price ?? 0, // ... })) ``` ### Pitfalls - **Missing `id` in mapping** — If the DB→frontend mapping omits `id`, `addItem()` in the cart will fail silently or create items that can't be removed - **Price nulls** — If the client's product list doesn't include prices, all products show "USD 0.00". Insert `price: null` and handle the display with "Consultar precio" instead of "USD 0.00" - **Category drift** — If `content/es.json` categories don't match the seeded categories, the filter UI shows categories with 0 products - **Build-time vs runtime** — If products are fetched at build time (SSG), new products won't appear until rebuild. Use `force-dynamic` or ISR with `revalidate = 60` ## WhatsApp Checkout from Cart For Paraguayan e-commerce sites without payment gateway credentials (Pagopar/Bancard), WhatsApp checkout is the primary conversion channel. Add it to the cart sidebar. ### Implementation In `components/cart-sidebar.tsx` (or equivalent cart drawer): ```tsx const phone = "[REDACTED_PHONE]" // real business number, no placeholders function buildWhatsAppMessage(items: CartItem[], total: number): string { const lines = items.map((item, i) => `${i + 1}. ${item.name} x${item.quantity} — ${formatPrice(item.price * item.quantity)}` ) return encodeURIComponent( `Hola! Quiero hacer un pedido desde la web de El Viajero:\n\n` + lines.join("\n") + `\n\n*Total: ${formatPrice(total)}*\n\n¿Cómo sigue el proceso de pago y envío?` ) } // Inside the cart sidebar JSX, near the checkout button: Pedir por WhatsApp ``` ### Key Points - Use `wa.me` links — they work on mobile and desktop without needing the WhatsApp API - Strip non-digits from the phone number for the `wa.me` URL - Pre-format the message with product list, quantities, and total — the business owner gets a complete order summary - Include a closing question ("¿Cómo sigue el proceso?") to prompt a response - Keep the button prominent (green, full-width) alongside any future payment gateway button - Also add "Consultar por WhatsApp" on individual product pages with a single-product message template ### Per-Product WhatsApp Button ```tsx Consultar por WhatsApp ```"}

{"action": "write_file", "name": "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "file_path": "references/supabase-product-seeding-pattern.md", "file_content": "# Supabase Product Seeding Pattern Reusable workflow for bulk-inserting real client product data into Supabase from a text list. ## When to Use - Client delivers a product list as text, spreadsheet, or photo of handwritten notes - Site currently has placeholder/demo products that must be replaced - Migrating from static JSON catalog to Supabase DB as source of truth ## Prerequisites - `psql` or `supabase-js` admin client with service_role key - Category normalization mapping agreed with client - Access to truncate and insert into the products table ## Workflow ### 1. Prepare the data Convert the client's list to a JS array of objects. Typical fields: ```typescript interface SeedProduct { name: string description?: string category: string // raw client input — will be normalized brand?: string stock: number price?: number // may be missing — handle gracefully price_before?: number // strikethrough "before" price sku?: string specs?: Record } ``` ### 2. Normalization function ```typescript const CATEGORY_MAP: Record = { "pezca": "Pesca", "pesca": "Pesca", "fishing": "Pesca", "electronica": "Electrónica", "Electronica": "Electrónica", "accesorios pers.": "Accesorios Personales", "accesorios personales": "Accesorios Personales", "equipo p/ auto": "Accesorios para Vehículos", "accesorios moto": "Accesorios para Vehículos", "camping": "Camping", "campamento": "Camping", } function normalizeCategory(raw: string): string { return CATEGORY_MAP[raw.trim().toLowerCase()] || raw.trim() } ``` ### 3. Truncate existing data ```bash # Via psql (direct DB connection) psql "$SUPABASE_DB_URL" -c "TRUNCATE TABLE ej_products RESTART IDENTITY CASCADE;" # Or via supabase-js admin client const { error } = await supabase.from('ej_products').delete().neq('id', 0) ``` ### 4. Insert script (Node.js with @supabase/supabase-js) ```javascript // scripts/seed-products.js require('dotenv').config({ path: '.env.local' }) const { createClient } = require('@supabase/supabase-js') const supabase = createClient( process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL, process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY // admin key, NOT anon key ) const products = [ // ... client's product array { name: "Anzuelo Mustad O'Shaughnessy", category: "Pesca", stock: 12, price: null }, { name: "Carpa Iglú 4 Personas", category: "Camping", stock: 5, price: null }, // ... 80 items ] const CATEGORY_MAP = { "pezca": "Pesca", "pesca": "Pesca", "electronica": "Electrónica", "accesorios pers.": "Accesorios Personales", "equipo p/ auto": "Accesorios para Vehículos", "accesorios moto": "Accesorios para Vehículos", "camping": "Camping", } function normalizeCategory(raw) { return CATEGORY_MAP[raw.trim().toLowerCase()] || raw.trim() } async function seed() { const rows = products.map((p, idx) => ({ name: p.name, description: p.description || null, category: normalizeCategory(p.category), brand: p.brand || null, stock: p.stock ?? 0, price: p.price ?? null, price_before: p.price_before ?? null, sku: p.sku || null, specs: p.specs || null, image_url: null, // images uploaded later status: p.stock > 0 ? 'active' : 'out_of_stock', created_at: new Date().toISOString(), updated_at: new Date().toISOString(), })) const { data, error } = await supabase.from('ej_products').insert(rows).select() if (error) { console.error('Insert failed:', error) process.exit(1) } console.log(`Inserted ${data.length} products`) // Verify category distribution const cats = {} rows.forEach(r => { cats[r.category] = (cats[r.category] || 0) + 1 }) console.log('Categories:', cats) } seed() ``` ### 5. Run and verify ```bash node scripts/seed-products.js ``` Verification queries: ```sql SELECT category, COUNT(*) FROM ej_products GROUP BY category ORDER BY count DESC; SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ej_products WHERE price IS NULL; -- how many need pricing SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ej_products WHERE image_url IS NULL; -- how many need images ``` ## Handling Missing Prices When the client's list doesn't include prices (common for initial data dumps): 1. Insert with `price: null` 2. In the frontend, render "Consultar precio" or "Precio a confirmar" instead of "USD 0.00" 3. In the admin panel, highlight rows with `price IS NULL` so the client knows what to fill ```tsx // Product card display {product.price != null ? formatPrice(product.price) : "Consultar precio"} ``` ## Handling Missing Images When products have no `image_url`: 1. Insert with `image_url: null` 2. Frontend shows a placeholder icon (e.g. `Package` from lucide-react) 3. Admin panel allows drag-and-drop image upload per product ```tsx // Thumbnail fallback {product.image_url ? ( ) : (
)} ``` ## Post-Seed Checklist - [ ] Build succeeds (`npm run build`) - [ ] Page renders products from DB, not static JSON - [ ] Category filters show the normalized names - [ ] Cart `addItem` works (each product has a valid `id` from DB) - [ ] No "USD 0.00" visible on frontend (handle null prices gracefully) - [ ] Deploy to production and verify live - [ ] Inform client which products need prices and images ## Category Normalization Pitfalls - **Case sensitivity**: "electronica" and "Electronica" must map to the same category - **Abbreviations**: "accesorios pers." needs explicit mapping — don't rely on fuzzy matching - **Combined categories**: "Equipo p/ Auto" and "accesorios moto" should often merge into a single category - **English inputs**: If client mixes Spanish and English ("fishing"), map both to the Spanish category name ## Re-seeding After Initial Load If the client sends an updated list later: 1. Decide: truncate + full re-seed (safest) or upsert by SKU 2. If upserting, ensure every product has a stable `sku` or `slug` as unique key 3. Always backup existing data before re-seeding: ```bash pg_dump "$SUPABASE_DB_URL" -t ej_products > backup_products_$(date +%Y%m%d).sql ``` "}
